# Securely store secrets for CI/CD pipelines

CI/CD pipelines require access to sensitive credentials such as API keys, database passwords, cloud provider credentials, and SSH keys. Native CI/CD secrets storage creates sprawl when teams duplicate credentials across platforms and environments, making rotation difficult and expanding the attack surface.

HashiCorp Vault provides a single source of truth for all CI/CD secrets. Vault integrates with major CI/CD platforms through OpenID Connect (OIDC) and JSON Web Token (JWT) authentication, removing long-lived static credentials.

## Why secure CI/CD secrets with Vault

Securing secrets in CI/CD pipelines addresses the following security and operational challenges:

**Remove secrets in pipeline logs:** CI/CD platforms log pipeline execution for debugging, but logs can capture secrets in plain text when commands echo credentials or API responses include sensitive data. Vault's dynamic secrets and secure retrieval methods prevent credentials from appearing in build output shipped to centralized observability platforms.

**Prevent secret sprawl:** Pipelines spanning build, test, and deploy stages across multiple environments require teams to duplicate secrets across CI/CD configurations. Vault centralizes secret storage so pipelines retrieve credentials on-demand, and a single Vault update propagates to all pipelines when a credential requires rotation.

**Automate credential rotation:** Dynamic secrets are generated per pipeline run with a short TTL, so a compromised credential expires automatically without requiring emergency rotation or manual updates to pipeline configurations.

**Audit secret access for compliance:** Vault's audit logging tracks every secret request with pipeline identity, timestamp, and accessed paths, providing the audit trail required for SOC 2, PCI DSS, and other compliance frameworks — a level of granularity not available in native CI/CD secrets stores.

The following diagram shows how Vault integrates with CI/CD platforms to provide centralized secrets management:

![Diagram showing how CI/CD platforms authenticate to Vault using OIDC and JWT to retrieve secrets centrally](/img/well-architected-framework/vault-cicd-1.png)

## Security best practices

Apply the following practices across all CI/CD platform integrations to strengthen your secrets management posture:

- **Use dynamic secrets over static when possible:** Dynamic secrets are generated on-demand and expire automatically, reducing the attack surface.
- **Enforce least-privilege policies:** Grant each CI/CD pipeline access only to the specific secrets it needs.
- **Enable audit logging:** Maintain a complete record of all secret access for compliance and security investigations.
- **Use OIDC/JWT authentication over static tokens:** Token-based authentication with OIDC eliminates long-lived credentials and provides fine-grained access control.
- **Rotate static secrets on a schedule:** For secrets that cannot be dynamically generated, implement automated rotation policies.

## Next steps

In this overview of managing CI/CD secrets, you learned that CI/CD pipelines require secure access to sensitive data and that you should use a centralized secrets store like HashiCorp Vault. Managing CI/CD secrets is part of the [Secure systems pillar](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems).

Choose a platform guide to integrate Vault with your CI/CD pipeline:

- [GitHub Actions](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/github-actions) for OIDC-based authentication with the Vault Action
- [GitLab CI/CD integration with Vault](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/gitlab) for JWT authentication with built-in Vault integration
- [Jenkins](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/jenkins) for AppRole, JWT, or LDAP authentication with the Vault plugin
- [CircleCI](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/circle-ci) for OIDC authentication using the Vault CLI
- [TeamCity](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/teamcity) for AppRole or LDAP authentication with Vault connections
- [Azure DevOps](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/azure-devops) for JWT/OIDC or managed identity authentication
- [Bitbucket Pipelines](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/bitbucket) for OIDC authentication using the Vault CLI
- [AWS CodeBuild](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/aws-codebuild) for AWS IAM-based authentication with native role integration
- [Google Cloud Build](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/google-cloud-build) for GCP IAM-based authentication with service accounts
- [Drone CI](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/drone-ci) for native Vault extension integration with access controls
- [Tekton](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/tekton) for Kubernetes authentication with Vault Secrets Operator
- [Argo CD](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/argo-cd) for Kubernetes authentication with Vault Secrets Operator
- [Terraform](/well-architected-framework/secure-systems/secure-applications/ci-cd-secrets/terraform) for OIDC authentication with native Terraform Cloud integration
